walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Boundary Stones
walkthrough.text: Let's get Schotten Totten set up. Take the nine stone markers and arrange them in a straight line between the two players. These stones represent the border between your Scottish clans. You will be fighting over each one by playing cards on your side.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Clan Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the fifty-four clan cards. These come in six different colors, and each color has cards numbered one through nine. Give them a thorough shuffle and place the deck face down to one side where both players can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Each player draws six cards from the deck to form their starting hand. Keep your cards hidden from your opponent. You will always have six cards in hand at the end of each turn, drawing one card to replace the one you played.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside Tactics Cards
walkthrough.text: If you are playing the Tactics variant, also called Battle Line, shuffle the ten tactics cards into a separate face-down deck. Place it next to the clan card deck. For your first game, we recommend playing without tactics cards to learn the core game first.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Stone Layout
walkthrough.text: Each stone has room for three cards on each side. You and your opponent will each play up to three cards on your side of each stone. The goal is to create the strongest three-card formation on your side. The player with the stronger formation claims that stone.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick who goes first by any method you like. The first player has a slight advantage because they act first, but the game is well-balanced. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Place 9 stone markers in a row between players

bullets: 54 clan cards: 6 colors × 9 numbers (1-9)
Shuffle and place as face-down draw pile

summary.title: Choose First Player
bullets: Choose randomly or by agreement
Slight first-player advantage but well-balanced

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Schotten Totten is a two-player card game where you battle over nine boundary stones. On each turn, you play one card to your side of any stone, then draw a card. Once three cards are on your side of a stone, you have a formation. The player with the stronger formation claims the stone. Win five stones total or three adjacent stones to win the game.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: On your turn, do three things in order. First, play one card from your hand to your side of any unclaimed stone. You can have at most three cards on your side of any stone. Second, optionally claim any stones where you can prove your formation wins. Third, draw one card from the deck to refill your hand.

walkthrough.title: Formation Rankings
walkthrough.text: The strongest is a Color Run, which is three consecutive numbers in the same color, like a red four, red five, red six. Second is Three of a Kind, three cards of the same number. Third is a Color, three cards of the same color. Fourth is a Run, three consecutive numbers in different colors. Weakest is Sum, which is any three cards with no pattern.

walkthrough.title: Breaking Ties
walkthrough.text: If both players have the same type of formation, the tie is broken by the sum of the card values. For a Color Run, the higher sum wins. For Three of a Kind, the higher number wins. For a Color or Run, the higher sum wins. For Sum formations, the higher total wins. If the formation type and sum are identical, the player who completed their formation first wins the stone.

walkthrough.title: Claiming Stones
walkthrough.text: Here is the clever part. You can claim a stone as soon as you can prove your formation is unbeatable. You do not need to wait for your opponent to play all three cards. If no remaining cards in the deck or your opponent's possible plays could form a stronger combination, you prove it and claim the stone immediately.

walkthrough.title: How to Prove a Claim
walkthrough.text: To prove a claim, look at all the cards that have been played across the entire board and what remains in the deck. Since you can see all played cards, you can deduce what your opponent could possibly play. If no combination of remaining cards could beat your formation at that stone, state your claim and explain why. Your opponent verifies, and if correct, you take the stone.

walkthrough.title: Win Conditions
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to win. First, claim any five of the nine stones. This is a majority victory. Second, claim three adjacent stones in a row, which is called a breakthrough. A breakthrough can happen earlier in the game since you only need three consecutive stones rather than five total.

walkthrough.title: Tactics Variant
walkthrough.text: Once you are comfortable with the base game, the Tactics variant adds ten special cards. On your turn, you may draw from either the clan deck or the tactics deck. Tactics cards have powerful effects like wild cards that represent any color or number, or abilities that remove an opponent's played card. The restriction is that you cannot play more tactics cards than your opponent has played plus one.

walkthrough.title: Empty Deck
walkthrough.text: If the draw pile runs out, play continues but players no longer draw cards. You simply play cards from your hand until someone wins. This creates tense endgame situations where every remaining card matters.

walkthrough.title: Public Information
walkthrough.text: All cards played to stones are face up and visible to both players. This means all information except your opponent's hand is public. Use this to your advantage by tracking which cards have been played and deducing what your opponent might hold.

Win: 5 stones (majority) or 3 adjacent (breakthrough)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice game between two players, Clan Red and Clan Blue. The nine stones are laid out between them, and each player has six cards in hand. Let's see how the battle unfolds.

walkthrough.title: Opening Moves
walkthrough.text: Clan Red starts by playing a green seven on stone five, the middle stone. Red is beginning to build a formation in the center. Clan Blue responds by playing a red nine on stone three. Both players are spreading out their initial plays.

walkthrough.title: Building Formations
walkthrough.text: Clan Red plays a green eight on stone five, pairing it with the green seven already there. Red is building toward a Color Run. A green six, seven, eight would be an incredibly strong formation. Clan Blue plays a red eight on stone three, starting a potential Color or Three of a Kind.

walkthrough.title: Clan Blue's Mistake: Tunnel Vision
walkthrough.text: Clan Blue has been focused entirely on stone three, playing a third card there to complete a red Color formation. Meanwhile, Blue has played zero cards on any other stone. Clan Red has been spreading plays across stones four, five, and six. Red is building three formations simultaneously while Blue has invested everything in one stone.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spread Your Plays
walkthrough.text: Do not commit all your cards to one stone early in the game. Your opponent can abandon that stone and focus on winning five others. Spread your plays across multiple stones, especially adjacent ones. This gives you more flexibility and makes it harder for your opponent to plan against you.

walkthrough.title: Clan Red Completes a Color Run
walkthrough.text: Clan Red draws the green six and plays it on stone five. Green six, seven, eight. That is a Color Run, the strongest possible formation. Clan Red looks at the board, sees that both green and non-green combinations cannot beat a Color Run of eighteen, and claims stone five.

walkthrough.title: The Claim Process
walkthrough.text: Red has a Color Run totaling twenty-one. For Blue to beat it, Blue would need a Color Run totaling twenty-two or higher. That would require a seven, eight, nine Color Run. Red checks the board. Are there any same-color seven, eight, nine combinations still possible with the remaining cards? If not, the claim is valid.

walkthrough.title: Clan Blue's Mistake: Not Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Clan Blue played a purple three on stone seven without thinking about what cards have already been played. Blue is trying to build a purple Color Run of three, four, five. But the purple four was already played by Red on stone two three turns ago. Blue's planned formation is now impossible. Two wasted cards.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track Played Cards
walkthrough.text: Always scan the board before playing a card. All played cards are face up, which means you have perfect information about what is no longer available. If a card you need for your formation has already been played elsewhere, your formation cannot be completed. Track the cards and adjust your plans accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Clan Red Pushes for Breakthrough
walkthrough.text: Red already has stone five. Now Red focuses on stones four and six, which are adjacent to stone five. If Red can claim both, that is three in a row, a breakthrough victory. Blue recognizes the threat and starts playing defensively on stones four and six.

walkthrough.title: Clan Blue's Mistake: Conceding a Lost Stone
walkthrough.text: Stone nine is a lost cause for Blue. Red has two high cards there and Blue has nothing. But instead of abandoning stone nine and focusing resources elsewhere, Blue plays a card on stone nine hoping to salvage it. That card would have been much more valuable on stone four where the battle is tight.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Sacrifice Hopeless Stones
walkthrough.text: When a stone is clearly lost, do not waste cards trying to save it. Redirect those cards to stones where you can actually win. Every card in your hand is precious. Playing a card on a lost stone is the same as throwing it away. Focus your resources where they will make a difference.

walkthrough.title: The Final Battle
walkthrough.text: The game comes down to stones four and six. Clan Red needs just one of them for the breakthrough. Clan Blue has managed to build a strong Run on stone four but Red's Color on stone six looks unbeatable. Red claims stone six using proof, completing the three-adjacent breakthrough. Game over.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great game! You saw three key lessons. First, spread your plays across multiple stones instead of going all-in on one. Second, track played cards so you do not pursue impossible formations. Third, sacrifice hopeless stones to focus resources where they matter. These habits will make you a much stronger player.

walkthrough.title: Next Steps
walkthrough.text:
Before your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for card counting and stone prioritization strategies.

Second, browse the Appendix for formation rankings and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Card Counting Is Essential
walkthrough.text: Schotten Totten has perfect information for all played cards. There are fifty-four cards in six colors, numbered one through nine. Every time a card is played, it is visible on the board. Get in the habit of scanning all nine stones before making your play. If you are chasing a specific card and it has already been played elsewhere, abandon that formation and pivot.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Color Runs
walkthrough.text: Color Runs are the strongest formation and should be your primary goal whenever possible. A Color Run beats every other formation type regardless of the numbers. When you have two cards of the same color in sequence, look for the third to complete the run. Even a low Color Run like one, two, three beats a Three of a Kind of nines.

walkthrough.title: Claim Stones Early
walkthrough.text: Many new players wait until both sides have three cards before claiming. But you can claim as soon as your formation is provably unbeatable. Early claims lock in stones and force your opponent to redirect their plans. Practice the claiming logic. Look at played cards, determine what is still available, and claim as soon as mathematically possible.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Adjacent Stones
walkthrough.text: Three adjacent stones win the game instantly, even if your opponent has more total stones. This means adjacent stones are more valuable than scattered ones. If you have a strong position on stones four and five, pour resources into stone three or six to complete the breakthrough. Force your opponent to defend multiple adjacent threats.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Abandon
walkthrough.text: If you have played one card at a stone and your opponent has a strong two-card start that you cannot beat, abandon it. Do not throw good cards after bad. Every card you waste on a losing stone is a card that could have won you a different stone. Learn to cut your losses early.

walkthrough.title: Opening Strategy
walkthrough.text: In the opening turns, play high-value cards on central stones where breakthroughs are possible. Avoid playing all your best cards on the edge stones since winning stones one and nine does not create breakthrough pressure. The middle stones, especially four, five, and six, are the most strategically valuable.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Watch where your opponent plays. If they are investing heavily in a specific stone, consider whether you can beat them there or if you should concede it. If they are spreading across three adjacent stones, they are likely going for a breakthrough and you need to block at least one. Their plays reveal their strategy.

walkthrough.title: Hand Management
walkthrough.text: With only six cards in hand, every draw matters. Think about not just your current play but what you need to draw in the future. If you are chasing a Color Run and need a specific card, consider the probability of drawing it. With fifty-four cards and six per hand, the math is manageable. Good hand management separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Positional Play
walkthrough.text: Advanced Schotten Totten is about controlling the board's shape, not just winning individual stones. By placing cards strategically, you can create situations where winning one stone automatically makes another stone claimable. For example, if claiming stone five frees up information that proves your stone six is unbeatable, you get a two-for-one. Plan your plays to create chain claims.

walkthrough.title: Probability-Based Decisions
walkthrough.text: With fifty-four cards and perfect knowledge of all played cards, you can calculate exact probabilities. If you need a specific card to complete a Color Run and there are twenty cards remaining in the deck, you have a one-in-twenty chance of drawing it. Factor in that your opponent might also be holding it. Good probability sense tells you when to chase a dream formation versus settling for a weaker but guaranteed one.

walkthrough.title: Bluffing and Misdirection
walkthrough.text: Even though there is no hidden board information, your hand is hidden. You can play cards that suggest you are building toward a specific formation to make your opponent waste cards defending it. Then redirect your actual strong cards elsewhere. For instance, play two medium cards of the same color at one stone as if you are going for a Color Run, forcing your opponent to respond, while your real attack is at an adjacent stone.

walkthrough.title: The Squeeze Play
walkthrough.text: A squeeze happens when your opponent needs specific cards to defend multiple stones but cannot get them all. Create pressure at three or four stones simultaneously. Your opponent is forced to choose which stones to save, and the ones they abandon become easy claims for you. The squeeze is most effective in the mid-game when hands are committed.

walkthrough.title: Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: Tempo in Schotten Totten means who is dictating the pace. If you are constantly forcing your opponent to react to your threats, you have tempo. Maintain it by making strong plays that demand a response. If your opponent has tempo, break it by making an unexpected play on a stone they are not focused on, forcing them to split attention.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Optimization
walkthrough.text: When the deck is nearly empty, the endgame shifts to pure hand management. Count the remaining cards carefully. Know exactly what your opponent could hold based on what has been played. In the final turns, every card placement should be calculated with full information. The player who manages the endgame transition best usually wins close games.

walkthrough.title: Tactics Card Strategy
walkthrough.text: In the Tactics variant, timing is everything. Wild cards are most powerful when they complete a Color Run on a contested stone. Card removal tactics are best used to break an opponent's nearly complete strong formation. Remember the restriction: you cannot play more tactics than your opponent plus one. This means using a tactics card early gives your opponent permission to use one too. Save tactics for pivotal moments.

entries.definition: One of nine stone markers placed in a line between players. Each stone is contested by both players placing cards. Claimed by the player with the stronger formation.
One of fifty-four cards used to form formations. Six colors with numbers one through nine in each color. These are the main cards used in gameplay.
One of ten special cards used in the Tactics or Battle Line variant. They have powerful effects like wild cards and card removal. Form a separate draw pile.
A set of three cards played on one side of a boundary stone. Formations are ranked by type and sum to determine which player claims the stone.
The strongest formation: three consecutive numbers in the same color (equivalent to a straight flush). Example: red 4-5-6. Ties broken by higher sum.
Second strongest formation: three cards of the same number in any colors. Example: three 7s. Ties broken by higher number.
Third strongest formation: three cards of the same color with no sequence requirement (equivalent to a flush). Ties broken by higher sum.
Fourth strongest formation: three consecutive numbers in different colors (equivalent to a straight). Example: blue 3, green 4, red 5. Ties broken by higher sum.
Weakest formation: any three cards that do not form a Color Run, Three of a Kind, Color, or Run. Winner determined by highest total card value.
The act of taking a boundary stone by proving your formation is unbeatable. Requires logical proof using all publicly visible card information.
A logical demonstration that no combination of remaining unplayed cards could form a stronger opposing formation at a given stone. Required to claim a stone.
Winning by claiming five of the nine boundary stones. This is the standard win condition.
Winning by claiming three adjacent boundary stones in a row. This can happen earlier than a majority victory and is a key strategic goal.
Stones that are directly next to each other in the line. Stones 4 and 5 are adjacent. Stones 4 and 6 are not. Adjacency matters for the breakthrough win condition.
The face-down deck of remaining clan cards. Players draw one card at the end of each turn to maintain a hand of six. When empty, play continues without drawing.
A tactics card that can represent any color and/or number when placed in a formation. The player declares what it represents when playing it.
A tactics card that allows you to remove one of your opponent's played cards from a stone. Powerful for breaking formations that are nearly complete.
You cannot have more tactics cards in play than your opponent has played plus one. This prevents one player from dominating through tactics cards alone.
